text: @JonathanMee - That's what I'm trying to tell you, you don't compare each element of `n` with every other element! That cannot happen. If you compare one element with a lot of other elements of `n`, then those "a lot of other elements" will be removed from the stack and nothing will be compared with them ever again. The number of times you have to compare a **fixed** element of `n` with other elements of `n` depends on the actual input, but the number of times you have to compare **all** elements of `n` with other elements of `n` is **linear in `n`**, so the whole thing is also linear.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains a logical error in algorithm complexity without mentioning any version changes or API deprecations.

text: It's not multiple threads getting more memory bandwidth, but using given bandwidth more efficiently by incurring fewer cache misses by having each thread reuse data brought in by another thread.  E.g., for a blocked matrix multiply A*B, bring in 2x2 block portions of A and B and use 4 threads to compute each  block of the 2x2 result.  Each block of A and B will be used twice, so only half the bandwidth was necessary for reading the input blocks compared to letting 4 threads work on arbitrary blocks.

text:
@Ernest yes ORDER BY is crucial in query above. 

DISTINCT ON removes duplicated rows using passed column list (in this example column list consist of `sections.section_name` only) to check whether rows are duplicated. For each group of duplicated records only the first one is left out, others are removed from final output. 

That's why we have to ensure that rows with `version_name` other than default (if present at all) appear before defaults.

Ordering by section_name is a postgresql constraint (columns used in DISTINCT ON have to appear in ORDER BY first). Really important is CASE clause.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains why the answer is correct and still relevant.

text: As is, after your `while` loop, `i` will have the same value as the last node's `next` pointer (which is NULL).  Then you set `i` to point at the new node... but that only modifies `i`, not the last node's `next` pointer.  Yes, you're leaving `*l` unchanged, and finding the end of the list, but you're not actually adding the new node because you don't modify the pointer at the end of the list -- just your local `i`.  That's why you need one more level of indirection to make it work... that or stop at the last node (rather than the last pointer) and set `i->next` instead of `i`.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains a logical error in pointer manipulation unrelated to time or version changes.

text: Don't use connection.end(); I think it's obsolete. This may not be the behaviour you want.  Connection.destroy() gets rid of the connection completely.  BTW the problem I was having was due to a completely separate part of the code making a query and not destroying it. I tracked it down with console.logs in the mysql code. ollama_explanation: The comment states that connection.end() is obsolete and recommends using connection.release() or connection.destroy() instead.

text: Talking about complexity for a fixed number of items doesn't make sense. Big-O is all about how the computation of time **changes** as a the size of the input **changes**.. For your question (4 being removed from an array of 6) that could mean you're always removing the last 4 items, no matter how big the array is in which case it's *O(1)*, or it could mean you're always removing the last 2/3 of the array in which case it's *O(n)*, or it could mean you're always removing all but the first two items (*O(n)*) again), etc.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains a fundamental concept of Big-O notation without indicating the answer is outdated or incorrect.

text: @chux: Form the very pedantic point of view, `%s` requires a pointer to the beginning of a *string*. *String* is a formal term in C defined in 7.1.1 as "a contiguous sequence of characters terminated by and including the first null character". The reason the spec of `s` specifier in `printf` does not use the term *string* is that it also has to cover such cases as `%42s`, which do not require null character termination. But pure `%s` requires a null terminator, which allows us to describe its argument as a pointer to a *string*.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains C string semantics without indicating the answer is outdated or incorrect.

text: Presumably RTRIM is both more desirable and more _correct_ for the intent than is TRIM. The _safest_ change is to explicitly replace **only** a _full stop_ as last character thus allowing the statement to run more than once; i.e. only multiple periods-as-suffix would be trimmed in later runs. text: When `LC2.DESCRIPTION` is `NULL` then `LC2.DESCRIPTION NOT LIKE 'obsolete'` isn't true. (`LC2.DESCRIPTION LIKE 'obsolete'` also isn't true.) text: Yep, I just checked and (in Nov 2017), **it _IS_ safe** to use the mongoose `update` function, while **it _IS NOT_ safe** to find a document, modify it in memory, and then call the `.save()` method on the document. Essentially in-memory manipulation is unsafe because you could be working on an outdated document so when you save, changes which occurred after the fetch step are lost.

text:
I know it's been a long time this has been posted, but for anybody finding this:
You should not use WeakMap for things labeled with strings (or numbers etc). String will never ever be cleared so the WeakMap will not free the memory once something is no longer used. Why? Because WeakMap frees the memory if the reference to the entry is completely gone. If you assign a key like `let key = 'myKey';` and then remove the key `key = null;`, you can still reassign it `key = 'myKey'` and it will match the reference.
ollama_explanation: The comment claims WeakMap behavior regarding string keys changed or was misunderstood, implying the answer is outdated due to this specific technical detail.

text: If anything probably the opposite: `_mm_malloc` *could* work by over-allocating and leaving metadata before the memory it returns, making it incompatible with `free` when the standard allocator isn't overalignment-capable. `posix_memalign` is compatible with `free` so it can't do that. http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/posix_memalign.3.html shows that on GNU/Linux, all the aligned-alloc functions are compatible with `free`.  The obsolete `memalign` *doesn't* provide any guaranteed-safe way to free the resulting memory, and did on some systems over-alloc and align without saving the pointer.
ollama_explanation: The comment explicitly states that `memalign` is obsolete and describes how older aligned-alloc functions were incompatible with `free`, indicating the answer's validity changed over time.

text: `__defineGetter__` remains in use and is therefore unlikely to be removed from browsers. If a browser implements __defineGetter__(), it also needs to implement the __lookupGetter__(), __lookupSetter__(), and __defineSetter__() methods.`

text: `CreateFileA` roughly performs the following operations: It allocates a `wchar_t` buffer, converts the pathname argument to Unicode, and calls `CreateFileW`. This adds overhead and is needlessly complex. The ANSI versions are really just there to ease porting of old code that was written for Win9x. Today, calling the wide character version is always the right thing to do. It may not be safer, but could be more reliable: If at some point the pathname winds up with characters outside the ASCII range, the conversion performed by `CreateFileA` may appear to fail on some systems.

text: In this instance you are wanting to pass some options to `browserify`. Using a streaming interface for this is not how you want to do it; you just want the options at runtime. In order for the second example to work, the `browserify` constructor should accept a stream of vinyl files, which it doesn't do. :-)
ollama_explanation: The comment explains that the filter fails because browserify outputs a single file, not a stream of vinyl files.

` is valid markup, but will probably (not certainly) be serialised with closing tags for the TR and TD. The serialisation may also include TBODY tags.

It doesn't matter if multiple requests come in from different clients, the V8 engine will queue up the requests and process them in order.

text: In SQL your comma-separated list is part of the statement and parsed as part of the statement. What you do in your PL/SQL function would be the same as if you in SQL did `select * from TEST where DEP_CODE in (:bindvariable)`. That would also fail with the same error. Your comma-separated list is *one* string value with the commas being part of the string, not part of the statement. If it did not work like that, then it would be impossible to search for string values in the data that contained commas within the string. You need to use a different method, what you are doing is not possible.
ollama_explanation: The comment explains why the specific approach fails due to SQL parsing rules rather than citing a change in technology or syntax.

text: @Jens: That won't work, because the current buffer is not synchronized; it's already got future data in it. Just changing `yyin` won't take effect until the end of the buffer is reached. Forcing a change will discard the data read. That's why `(f)lex` provides buffers and buffer stacks; you should use them. Also, there is absolutely no issue with setting the start condition whenever you want to, even in an EOF rule, because it is *global*, not part of the buffer state. (In a pure lexer, it is part of the scanner state. But there is only one per scanner.)
ollama_explanation: The comment explains why a specific approach fails due to internal buffer mechanics rather than citing an outdated version or API change.

text: No, the *caller* has the least restrictions for `Fn`: e.g. they can call it through a `&Fn()`, but neither `FnMut` or `FnOnce` can be called there, while `FnOnce` has to be called by-value and can only be called once (which is much more restrictive). On the otherhand, `Fn` places the most restrictions on the *callee* (the closure itself), while `FnOnce` is the least restrictive for the closure (that is the body of a `FnOnce` closure has the most flexibility). As the answer describes, `FnOnce` is the best, but there's some subtleties around trait objects that mean it doesn't work directly.
ollama_explanation: The comment clarifies trait object restrictions without indicating the answer is outdated or incorrect.

text: Using $parent is usually bad style. I.e. using $parent.search() is nonsence unless u have two functions with same name 'search' in different scopes. Otherwise just use simple search() - it will find function in parent scope. Same is for $parent.error - u can use just error as u read variables. The only thing that is not working - changing parent scope variable from child scope. (This is $parent.location here) So u may sometimes use $parent for this. text: The reason your first attempt did not work was that the `announcementObject` was bound to the controller instance instead of the scope. With directives you shouldn't typically bind to the controller instance. The more configurable approach is to set up the isolated binding  with the parent controller scope. That way your parent controller can call a service to get the announcements, and add them to the scope. Once they are added to the scope, the directive will update as well, since we specified the two-way binding when we set `<announcement-block-update announcement="announcement"></ann..`.

text: This will not work with most versions of `find`: [according to POSIX](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695399/utilities/find.html): _Only a plus sign that follows an argument containing the two characters "{}" shall punctuate the end of the primary expression. Other uses of the plus sign shall not be treated as special._ Even GNU `find` complains about this and doesn't accept the command. That's why some versions of `cp` have the `-t` (target directory) option: `find "$from" -name "*.$ext" -exec cp -t "$dest" {} +`.

text: Make compiles code to create executables that can be run.  Make doesn't _write_ the code, it just compiles it. A value may not even be evaluated at all, if a directive decides to use it directly.

text: Another wild guess: If you're using Mac OS X or a BSD, those come with BSD sed, which expects a filename extension for the backup file as parameter to `-i`. It would treat `s/.../.../` as the filename extension and the filename as sed code. I believe it allows an empty extension to avoid the generation of a backup file, so you could try `-exec sed -i '' "s/$input_string/$replace_string/" '{}' \;`; if that doesn't work, `-exec sed -i.bak "s/$input_string/$replace_string/" '{}' \; -exec rm '{}.bak' \;` will do the trick (if no `foo.bak` files that need to be preserved are in the directory tree).

text: I agree that Optimistic Concurrency is better to use in general, for the specific case of getting records to process ('tasks') it does not work because all concurrent transactions pick the same record to process. The result is that there is always a conflict for all processors, except the first one that wins. For Optimistic Concurrent to work there has to be a low probability of conflict, it works great when the record being updated is basically random, but fail when the record being updated is deterministic (ie. everyone picks the 'next task').
ollama_explanation: The answer fails specifically when updates are deterministic rather than random.

text: The first paragraph of this answer is correct, but the second paragraph is completely wrong. It is *not* necessary to call `select` on a socket before calling `recv` a second time.  If you only have one socket to receive from, there is no harm in blocking in `recv` instead of `select`.  If you have more than one, then you of course have set all of them to nonblocking, and you can -- indeed, should -- call `recv` in a loop until it fails with `errno` set to `EAGAIN`.
